description.en: Germany's old army-ration loaf: a straight rye sourdough baked low and slow in a tin until the crust turns thick and dark. Built for durability, not delicacy - it once had to survive weeks in a soldier's pack and still taste of something.
description.de: Das alte deutsche Kommissbrot: ein reiner Roggensauerteig, lang und mäßig heiß im Kastenbrot gebacken, bis die Kruste dick und dunkel wird. Für Haltbarkeit gemacht, nicht für Zartheit – einst wochenlang im Tornister haltbar.
description.es: El viejo pan de ración del ejército alemán: una masa madre de centeno puro, horneada despacio en molde hasta que la corteza queda gruesa y oscura. Pensado para durar, no para ser delicado, antaño aguantaba semanas en la mochila del soldado.
description.fr: Le vieux pain de munition de l'armée allemande: un levain de seigle pur, cuit longuement en moule jusqu'à une croûte épaisse et sombre. Conçu pour durer, pas pour la finesse, autrefois capable de tenir des semaines dans le sac d'un soldat.

origin.note: Kommiss was slang for military supply; Kommissbrot was the standard-issue ration loaf of German-speaking armies for centuries, prized for surviving long storage.

ingredients.note: Roggenmehl Type 1370, split between sourdough and main dough

label.es: malta de centeno tostada (para el color)
label.fr: malt de seigle torréfié (pour la couleur)

keeping.note: Keeps up to two weeks at room temperature; let it rest a full day before the first slice.

summary.en: Mix rye flour, lukewarm water and starter; ferment covered about 12 hours, starting slightly warm and cooling gradually.

Combine the ripe sourdough with the remaining rye flour, water, salt, beet syrup, roasted rye malt and a little fresh yeast into a thick, plaster-like mass. There is no real kneading, just thorough mixing.
Cover and let the dough relax 30-40 minutes so the rye flour fully hydrates.
Wet your hands, tools and the work surface, then roll the dough into a log and ease it into a greased tin, smoothing the top with a wet scraper.
Dust with rye flour and let rise about 70-80 minutes at room temperature until it reaches the rim and shows small surface cracks.
Bake hot, then step the oven down and continue until deeply crusted, about 70-75 minutes total.
Cool completely, then let the loaf sit at least a day; the dense crumb needs that time to firm up for clean slices.

source.adaptation: Method paraphrased; used the recipe's regular fresh-yeast option (8g) rather than its organic-yeast variant (16g).
